OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":22,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"carpenter","city_of_living":"Philadelphia","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"primary school","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"creative","religious_beliefs":"Hinduism","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["photography","reading novels","painting"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":81,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":69,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":51,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":81,"level":"high"},"neuroticism":{"score":56,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"As a 22‑year‑old carpenter who spends a lot of time scrolling through feeds while my toddler naps, I’ve learned that fake content is like a splinter hidden in a freshly cut plank—easy to miss until it starts to hurt. The first step is to treat every headline as a work of art that needs to be inspected. I cross‑check a claim by looking for the source: is it a reputable news outlet, a government agency, or a random page that posted a sensational screenshot? I’ve built a habit of opening the original link, if any, and seeing whether the author’s credentials line up with the story. If the article is shared by many of my friends, I still pause; popularity is not proof of truth. When I find a shaky source, I use a quick fact‑checking tool like Snopes or the Fact‑Check section on Reuters to see if the claim has been debunked. If it’s still unclear, I add a note in my phone’s notes app—“Check later” or “Unverified”—so I don’t let it linger in my mind.

I also rely on the visual clues that my photography hobby has trained me to notice. A grainy image, inconsistent lighting, or a watermark that looks fake can be red flags. If a post is too good to be true, I ask myself if the author’s tone is emotional rather than balanced. The principle of “when in doubt, verify” has become a part of my routine, much like checking the dimensions of a piece of wood before cutting.

On the social side, I’ve started to curate my feed by unfollowing accounts that frequently circulate misinformation. I also turn on the “safety” or “trusted” filters on platforms that flag questionable content. Finally, I share only content that I’ve verified, and I add a brief note explaining my source. This practice not only protects my family’s understanding of the world but also reinforces the values of honesty and diligence that I bring to my carpentry work.

Managing fake content is not a one‑time fix; it’s an ongoing discipline that mirrors the careful attention I give to each board I join. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
As a 22‑year‑old carpenter who spends a lot of time scrolling through feeds while my toddler naps, I’ve learned that fake content is like a splinter hidden in a freshly cut plank—easy to miss until it starts to hurt. The first step is to treat every headline as a work of art that needs to be inspected. I cross‑check a claim by looking for the source: is it a reputable news outlet, a government agency, or a random page that posted a sensational screenshot? I’ve built a habit of opening the original link, if any, and seeing whether the author’s credentials line up with the story. If the article is shared by many of my friends, I still pause; popularity is not proof of truth. When I find a shaky source, I use a quick fact‑checking tool like Snopes or the Fact‑Check section on Reuters to see if the claim has been debunked. If it’s still unclear, I add a note in my phone’s notes app—“Check later” or “Unverified”—so I don’t let it linger in my mind.

I also rely on the visual clues that my photography hobby has trained me to notice. A grainy image, inconsistent lighting, or a watermark that looks fake can be red flags. If a post is too good to be true, I ask myself if the author’s tone is emotional rather than balanced. The principle of “when in doubt, verify” has become a part of my routine, much like checking the dimensions of a piece of wood before cutting.

On the social side, I’ve started to curate my feed by unfollowing accounts that frequently circulate misinformation. I also turn on the “safety” or “trusted” filters on platforms that flag questionable content. Finally, I share only content that I’ve verified, and I add a brief note explaining my source. This practice not only protects my family’s understanding of the world but also reinforces the values of honesty and diligence that I bring to my carpentry work.

Managing fake content is not a one‑time fix; it’s an ongoing discipline that mirrors the careful attention I give to each board I join. By staying curious, cross‑checking, and filtering my feed, I keep my digital environment as sturdy as the homes I build.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Identified key persona traits: age, occupation, social media usage, hobbies, high openness, conscientiousness, agreeableness, moderate neuroticism. Chose tone: friendly, practical, reflective, with creative references to photography and carpentry. Structured opinion as continuous prose, 300+ words, no bullet points. Included concrete steps: source checking, fact‑checking, visual clues, filtering feed, sharing verified content. Added citations array with relevant organizations.
